videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Buenos Aires, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So let's hope you enjoy it! Buenos Aires is the vibrant capital city of Argentina. It sits on the western bank of the Rio De La Plata, a vast estuary on the eastern coast of South America that was once crucial for trade. Founded in the 16th century as a Spanish Colonial Outpost, the city grew from a military base to a major commercial center, becoming a melting pot of cultures. Today, Buenos Aires is renowned for its European-influenced architecture, passionate tango scene, and delicious cuisine. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in and around the city. At the end, we'll also share a few travel tips to help you have a safe and enjoyable time. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Located in an upscale area of the city, La Recoleta Cemetery is like a grand outdoor museum. Elaborate mausoleums, sculpted crypts, and ornate monuments adorn the grounds, showcasing the wealth of Argentina's elite, particularly during the 19th and 20th centuries. Among the notable residents are Evita Perón, whose tomb is a major pilgrimage site. You'll also find monuments of countless politicians, artists, and intellectuals. Consider taking a guided tour to learn more about the history and significance of the cemetery as well as its inhabitants. It's also worth strolling around the Recoleta neighborhood as it's known for its stunning architecture. La Boca is a vibrant port neighborhood in Buenos Aires. It's known for having many colorful houses, which stems from early Italian immigrants who repurposed leftover paint from ships to brighten their homes. The most popular street to visit is Caminito, where street performers, tango dancers, and artisans create a lively atmosphere. Another highlight is Fundación Proa, a contemporary art museum. While this tourist area is generally safe during the day, it's important to exercise caution here, especially at night. If you're interested in watching a football match, you can also check the schedule online to see if the the famous Boca Juniors will be playing in their stadium nearby. Imagine visiting another country as a day trip. You can do that with a 1 hour 15-minute ferry ride across the Rio De La Plata, which brings you to Colonia del Sacramento in Uruguay. This was the first UNESCO World Heritage Site in the country, and was founded in 1680 by a Portuguese explorer. Once you exit the ferry terminal, take a short walk to enter the town center through the old city walls. The architecture inside the Old Town blends both Portuguese and Spanish styles. Stroll along its cobblestone streets, admire the colorful houses, see the iconic lighthouse, and enjoy the peaceful local restaurants. The streets here are really peaceful and quiet, which is a nice break from Buenos Aires. And you can also find these really cool vintage cars all across the historic center. The Marcado de San Telmo is an indoor market housed in a historic building. This bustling market offers a diverse range of goods, from fresh produce and local delicacies to artisanal crafts, antiques, and clothing. Whether you're seeking a delicious lunch or unique souvenirs, the Mercado de San Telmo is a great place to explore. The surrounding San Telmo neighborhood with its historic square and vibrant atmosphere is also a fun area to explore. On Sundays, the market spills onto the streets and you might get lucky to see an outdoor tango show. Puerto Madero is a modern district located along the eastern waterfront of Buenos Aires. This neighborhood is renowned for its lively atmosphere and picturesque canal, which winds through the district and offers scenic views. We recommend strolling along the waterfront promenade, where you can enjoy the cool breezes and cityscape or grab a drink at one of the many elegant riverside bars. While Puerto Madero is pleasant during the day, the evenings is when this neighborhood becomes truly magical, with amazing restaurants offering international and local cuisine. The Ecological Reserve is an expansive green oasis located on the eastern edge of Puerto Madero. This unique urban reserve boasts diverse ecosystems, including lagoons, grasslands, and woodlands. Here, you can enjoy leisurely walks along well-maintained trails while spotting a variety of bird species. It's a a great place to enjoy some quiet time away from the noise of the city. This park is free to enter, but make sure you check the closing times because it's not open 24/7. Palermo is one of the most vibrant neighborhoods in Buenos Aires, offering a diverse range of accommodations, dining, and nightlife. Palermo Soho lies at the heart of its trendy scene, centered around Plaza Serrano. This plaza is an eclectic hub of bars and clubs. The surrounding streets in this area are teeming with trendy restaurants, offering a global culinary experience. Checking out a speakeasy is also something that you can't miss in this neighborhood. We particularly enjoyed a place called Nicky Harrison, where a delicious sushi dinner led to a hidden bodega for a unique drinking experience. You can find a link to our blog post in the description below the video, which includes a map of bars, restaurants, and speakeasies in Buenos Aires. Tango is a dance that was born in the impoverished neighborhoods of Buenos Aires in the late 19th century and is an essential part of Argentinian culture. You can encounter it everywhere around the city, from free performances in plazas to paid shows at theaters. While spontaneous street performances offer a taste of the local vibe, a professional show provides a more well-rounded experience. We witnessed this at the Teatro Piazzolla, where tango performances were accompanied by talented vocalists. The music, the dancers' passion, and the historic theater setting made for an impressive 1-hour show. Commonly known as the Bosques de Palermo, the Tres de Febrero Park is the largest green space in the city. The park boasts a range of woodlands, lakes, squares, and different walking paths that extend 1.5 square miles or 3.7 square kilometers. Enjoy a leisurely stroll, rent a bike, or simply relax on the grass while soaking in the vibrant atmosphere. The park is a beloved spot for locals, creating a lively and authentically Argentinian experience. Founded in 1580, Plaza de Mayo is the heart of the historic center. This central square has witnessed some of the most pivotal moments in Argentina's modern history, from political protests to celebrations. Today, you can find the pink Presidential Palace, the Metropolitan Cathedral, and the former Seat of the colonial government here. Other notable landmarks in the historic center include the National Congress, Evita Perón's mural, and the Palacio Barolo. If you want to delve deeper into the city's history and culture, we recommend going on a free walking tour. You can find a link to the tour we joined in the description below the video. As we promised at the start of the video, we'll now share a few travel and safety tips for Buenos Aires. The city has an excellent public transportation system, including an extensive subway and bus network. These are generally affordable and efficient, especially during off-peak hours. Uber and Cabify are also popular options, but availability can be limited. Traffic congestion can significantly impact travel times. Since distances between attractions can be far, factor in travel time when planning your itinerary. Choosing your base in Buenos Aires depends on your travel style and budget. Some good areas include Palermo, Puerto Madero, Recoleta, San Telmo and downtown. If you prefer a central location, consider staying in the historic center. You should also check the distances to your points of interest when deciding where to stay since the main things to do in the city are spread out. As in any major city, exercise caution and be mindful of your surroundings anywhere you go. Be wary of pickpocketing, especially in crowded areas. While most areas are safe for tourists, be extra aware in certain parts of the city, particularly at night. These areas include parts of San Telmo, La Boca, areas around Retiro train station and Constitución. If you have more time in the city or are looking for other things to do, we we put a list of more options on the screen here for you. And if you're planning your trip to Argentina, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included other important links in the description that will help with your Buenos Aires experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

The subway system is home to countless thieves. The trains make it easy for pickpockets to bump into you and then vanish into the crowd. I do recommend taking the trains for their ease of use and low cost, but be very careful with your belongings. Our next scam is one of the most prevalent scams in most of South America, and it's the classic taxi meter scam. Any time you enter a cab, make sure the driver turns on the meter as he begins to drive. If they tell you the meter is broken. Exit the taxi and find a different cab. It may also be a good idea to use the GPS on your phone to ensure the driver is taking the correct route. While my friends, both solo men and women, took many taxis across Argentina, you may find it safer to take an Uber or use another ridesharing service. Our third pickpocket hotspot takes us to the sand Telmo neighborhood for its famous Sunday fare. The area de San Telmo is home to hundreds of vendors and sees thousands of visitors on the weekend. The combination of chaotic streets and massive swarms of tourists with cash make this a prime location for thieves. A quick intermission and a reminder now, if you saw my earlier video about scams in Barcelona and read some of the comments. So many people are saying, I'm never going to go to Barcelona again. I'm never going to go here again. Please do not feel that way about coming to Argentina. I have felt incredibly safe during my, what, 75 days here. I've also talked to dozens, close to 100 travelers who have happily traveled throughout this country without incident. But it's because of the few I have met that have experienced crime here that I'm making this video for you here today. Not so that you avoid this country. Just that you can do it as safely as possible. And I know I've reiterated that a few times, but I really want to hit that part home. Argentina is definitely one of the safer countries in Latin America. I'm about a block away from the Plaza de Maggio on a street called Florida, one of the most popular and busiest streets in Buenos Ayers for both tourists and locals. But I like to call it the Cambio Corner. This can also be called the black market of B. A. When you walk up and down this street, it's impossible to not hear the word cambio, cambio cambio on your. Yo, yo, yo. Garfield confused. You, guy. You're going to hear so many people shouting it from every corner of the street. No joke. There literally must be over 100 people out here just yelling Cambio. After you walk down this block, you're never going to want to hear the word cambio again in your life. It's almost annoying how often people say it and how many people there are here saying it. Whatever you do, do not exchange any of your foreign currency here on the black market. I've heard of the cambio people giving a lower rate than is actually advertised and worst of all, I've actually heard of them giving counterfeit bills. So in summary, please do not exchange your money on the street. Wester in Union is the most efficient, the safest and also the best value when it comes to transferring and exchanging your money here in Argentina. Kind of a side note, if someone on the street is saying, my friend, my friend. Always ignore them. Nothing good can come of responding to that. It's usually they're trying to sell you something or some narcotic or something even worse. So don't pay attention to anyone that says My friend, my friends. Tune them out on my walk through here. I've also had a few people try to sell me some illicit substances. Do not buy any of those. Please let me know if there's any scams here that I've missed. I didn't cover a lot of like common sense tips and other ways to stay safe. If you want to see a video like that, a video on how to travel as safely as possible, let me know in the comments. More than happy to put something together like that. That kind of encompasses all the scams you're going to find in most countries throughout the world. And just general tips on staying safe while traveling as well. So if you want that, let me know in the comments. And that's that. I really hope you enjoyed this video as much as you enjoyed my other videos about traveling throughout Argentina. My goal is to inspire you to travel, to go to places you've never been, but to also ensure that your trip there is done as safely as possible. And hopefully this video helped you with that just a little bit. Remember, so many of the crimes and scams that we talked about in today's video can happen in any country in the world. And just because I'm talking about them happening here doesn't mean they're going to happen to you. As always, the best precaution against crime is to use straight of common sense. If you want to see more videos about scams and crimes in other countries, let me know. In about 16 hours I'm about to hop on a plane and head to El Salvador, a country that certainly has many misconceptions about it, especially when it comes to safety. So I can't wait to walk you through there and show you how to travel through El Salvador as safely as possible. But for now, my name's Brent Timm from Argentina. And I'm saying ciao for now.

videos.transcript: [Music] Buenos Aires is the sprawling capital of Argentina and by far the country's most populous area. With over 3 million people living in the city, which increases to over 13 million if you include the surrounding metropolitan area, Buenos Aires is home to just over a third of Argentina's entire population. It is one of the most visited cities in Latin America, with a distinctly European influence in its architecture and culture, leading to it being given the nickname the Paris of South America. We spent two weeks in Buenos Aires, in which time we explored many of its popular neighborhoods, visited countless impressive landmarks and sampled many of Argentina's most famous foods. If you're new here we're Kitti and Jon and we love to travel and experience new cultures. Whether exploring our own backyard in the UK or watching volcanoes erupt in Central America, our passion for travel never seems to fade. Recently we've been spending a lot of our time in South America and have really fallen in love with the continent, so make sure to Subscribe and hit that Notification Bell if you'd like to see more content from this beautiful part of the world. Without further ado, let's get into the video. Buenos Aires is seriously huge and, if it's your first time in the city, it can be pretty daunting trying to figure out where to stay. But there are a few neighborhoods that we would recommend based on both safety and reducing your travel time to other parts of the city. We stayed in Recoleta home to the world famous cemetery and sandwiched between the popular areas of Palermo to the west and Monserrat and San Telmo to the south. We enjoyed this central location and the Recoleta district itself is very safe and full of museums to explore and green parks to relax in. We paid about $38 per night for a nice spacious apartment here. Any of the other neighborhoods we mentioned would also make good bases though. Palermo is home to the trendy Palermo Soho area, as well as countless beautiful parks to explore. Monserrat is the historic heart of the city, where you'll find many of the grandest buildings and San Telmo gives off a distinctly bohemian vibe, with antique stores and cafes on every corner. Wherever you decide to base yourself, getting around Buenos Aires couldn't be easier. The city has a widespread and very efficient public transport system including buses, subway and trains, so you can get literally anywhere you want to within the metropolitan area. Argentina is actually the first country we've been to that has a universal travel card that you can use across the entire country. First you'll need to pick up a SUBE card at any subway stations or eligible Kiosco's and load it either using cash or the app. It was easier for us to top the SUBE card up with cash than download the app because our apps couldn't find it because our phones are from the UK and not from Argentina. Also just a word of warning: it's better to top up your SUBE card in the morning because some of the stores get only a certain amount of credit and once they use that you won't be able to top up your card with the amount that you would want to. Once you've loaded your card you can just tap it and use it on any of the buses or subways or trains. We personally used buses more often than any other mode of transportation because they were very regular and they took us wherever we wanted to go. Just like with any major city, Buenos Aires is made up of lots of different neighborhoods and they all have certain things and features that help differentiate them from each other. In this video we're going to try and explore as many as possible to give you an idea of some of the best neighborhoods to visit whilst you're here in Buenos Aires. [Music] As we were staying in Recoleta we decided to begin our Buenos Aires exploration there. Recoleta is an affluent neighborhood with many Parisian style town houses and pleasant parks to wander around but by far it's biggest draw is the Recoleta Cemetery. Just like in Xela in Guatemala our first stop here in Buenos Aires is actually a cemetery. One of the most popular places to come in Recoleta because it is beautiful, like the mausoleums here are gorgeous. They really do remind us of the Xela ones, just a little bit less colorful. One big difference between this cemetery and the one in Xela though is that this one is a lot more expensive. I mean the Xela one was free to enter, this one was a whopping $16 per person to come in. Definitely the most expensive cemetery we've ever been to. Built in 1822, the cemetery contains over 4,000 vaults from simple mausoleums to ones that look like mini mansions. Many notable people are buried there: Presidents of Argentina and Nobel Prize winners, as well as Argentina's First Lady Eva Peron. It's been voted as one of the world's most beautiful cemeteries on numerous occasions and is laid out like city blocks, with wide treelined avenues branching off into smaller side areas lined with mausoleums of all shapes and sizes. Certainly worth your time but a little overpriced for foreigners in our opinion. From the cemetery we walked down through the park to the nearby Museo De Bellas Artes. It's free to enter and houses one of the largest collections of public art in Latin America. The ground floor is filled with much older works, which we definitely enjoy more, whereas the second floor is made up of much more Contemporary and Modern Art, which we don't really get! After admiring the artwork we headed across the colorful bridge next to the gallery, where you get nice views of the Facultad de Derecho, part of the University of Buenos Aires, and then continued to the park next door to see the steel Floralis Generica sculpture. Although it didn't look at its best when we were there as the water in the reflecting pool underneath wasn't there and two of the petals were missing. The final must visit spot in Recoleta is the El Ateneo bookstore. This is no ordinary bookstore though. It's housed inside a converted 20th Century theater, which makes it probably the coolest, if not one of the most touristy, bookshops we've ever visited. Bookshelves line all the balconies and ground floor seating areas and the stage has been converted into a cafe. Just be prepared to pay a premium if you want to buy a book there. Although it's not really in Recoleta but the bordering San Nicolas District, the Teatro Colon is so close to the bookstore that you might as well try to see it in the same day. Located in the center of the huge Plaza Lavalle, this theater is known for having some of the best acoustics in the world. So if it's within your budget you should definitely try to catch a show there. Bordering Recoleta to the north, Palermo is a huge district made up of lots of smaller areas and the largest collection of parks in the city. We began the day in the trendy Palermo Soho area, where you'll find countless bars and cafe's along leafy cobblestone streets, making it a great place to come in the evenings. It's still worth wandering around during the day though because it's one of the best areas in Buenos Aires to see some street art. Pasaje Russel is definitely the highlight, with every spare piece of wall covered with one beautiful mural or another. [Music] Once we were done taking pictures, we headed to the north of the district and the first of many parks there. The Botanical Garden is free to wander around and is home to a gorgeous greenhouse and many rare plants from around the world. [Music] Then we headed through the Buenos Aires Eco Parque, probably the most unique park in the city. It's a former zoo so you'll be walking along paths through former animal enclosures, some of which still contain the animals. Let's just say we were a little bit surprised when we saw a couple of giraffes there! Animals here have been rescued from captivity so can't be released back into the wild for various reasons. They're used for education and awareness. Many of the animals though are allowed to wander freely around the park. At the bottom of this park we crossed over into the Parque Rosedal which, as you might have guessed, contained many, many roses. Other than our initial walk through that Palermo Soho area here in Palermo today, the majority of the time has just been walking through the different parks of Palermo. This whole area near the coast is just super green and it just seems to go on forever, like we're now currently in the Rose Garden but we've passed through Eco Parks, through botanical gardens, we've still got the Japanese Garden to go. Super, super green area of the city and really nice to walk around and escape the city noise. [Music] After relaxing in the Rose Garden for a bit we walked our very tired legs across to the Japanese Garden. This was the one park that required an entry fee but at 4,500 pesos per person it was pretty reasonable. We always love walking around a Japanese Garden because there's just something so relaxing and peaceful about them, even when they're a little bit busy with tourists! [Music] If you're in the mood for some more art, then definitely check out the MALBA Museum for a look at some more contemporary Latin American works. So the best part of coming to Buenos Aires in November is that all the jacaranda trees are blooming at this time and they paint the whole city like this purply, blue color. Absolutely beautiful I love it here! There are many streets where you can just walk around and enjoy these trees. I mean now they're already falling on the ground so we kind of just got here at the right time. One literally just fell on my hand! A little further south are the contrasting neighborhoods of Puerto Madero and Monserrat. Puerto Madero is the new, revamped dockside area of the city, whereas Monserrat is its historic heart. In Puerto Madero we found sleek skyscrapers, reminding us of London's Canary Wharf, alongside renovated red brick dock buildings housing upscale restaurants, a little bit like the Royal Albert Docks in Liverpool. In short it kind of felt like we were back home in the UK! Make sure to walk along the impressive Puente de la Mujer for nice views back towards the city skyline. The rest of Puerto Madero is made up of the Costanera Sur Ecological Park, which has looping hiking trails and lots of wildlife but we didn't have time to walk around it during our time in the city. We then made our way into Monserrat, with the first stop being the city's main square: Plaza de Mayo. You'd be hard pushed to miss the bright pink facade of the Casa Rosada, which is the Presidential Palace of Argentina, at the head of the square. The rest of the square is surrounded by more grand buildings like the National Bank of Argentina and the Metropolitan Cathedral of Buenos Aires. Close by is the famous and extremely popular Cafe Tortoni, an iconic French style cafe that has been open since 1858. The line is always long for a table and we weren't patient or hungry enough at that time to stick around! Then we crossed Avenida 9 de Julio, the widest avenue in the world with 16 lanes of traffic flowing along it. If you want you can also head up it a short way to check out the Obelisk, which was unveiled in 1936 and is made of Cordoba white stone. On the other side we headed towards Plaza del Congreso. This plaza is bordered by the Palacio Barolo to the east, where you can take a tour and have a drink at the rooftop restaurant. Dominating the west end of the square is the enormous National Congress of Argentina, which is very reminiscent of the US Congress Building in Washington DC. Monserrat was definitely not our favorite neighborhood to explore, as it was just a little bit too busy and hectic for our liking but it is a must visit if you want to see some of the the grandest buildings in the city and some of its most historic places. [Music] Just south of Monserrat is the bohemian district of San Telmo. It's a fairly small area but is absolutely packed full of antique shops, where you can find pretty much anything you could imagine. You'll find cafes and eateries on every corner as you wander around the cobblestone streets. The entire district surrounds Plaza Dorrego, where you'll find craft fairs on weekends and, if you're lucky, spot Tango dancers showing off their moves for locals and tourists. Make sure to also take a stroll through the Mercado de San Telmo, to browse a wide selection of artisan goods and sample some delicious looking food. When we were there it was full of Brazilian football fans in town for a final, which gave us an idea of the passion South American fans show in support of their teams! [Applause] Casa Ezeiza is worth a quick look if you're in the market for souvenirs or antiques and didn't find what you were looking for elsewhere, and Casa Minima, which is the thinnest house in Buenos Aires. It can be easy to miss if you're not paying attention! From San Telmo we headed to La Boca, the final and probably the most touristy district of the city that we recommend visiting during your stay. This is a working class neighborhood and had much more in common with the South America that we were used to seeing in places like Colombia and Peru. That's not to say that it didn't have its own unique feel though. Coloful murals adorned every single building and surface and, if not a mural, then it was just painted one bright color or another. It also felt a little like being at the circus, as the signs over the shops and in the streets were very reminiscent of what you might see at a European circus. La Boca is also home to Boca Juniors, one of the biggest football teams in Argentina, and the brightly colored stadium dominates the skyline. If you're lucky enough to be in town on a match day and get tickets, this is definitely a must do activity in Buenos Aires. Football culture is almost a religion in Argentina and we saw evidence of this painted across the walls and stores of La Boca. Two players, Messi and Maradona, definitely get the most love though and you will see their likeness everywhere you look. We also found lots of small little alleyways coming off the main avenue filled with souvenir and antique stores, with colorful signs and exteriors. So it's a great place to do some shopping if you still haven't managed to find the perfect souvenir. La Boca is popular for a reason and it's definitely a must visit district in Buenos Aires, although a couple of hours was more than enough for us before we started to feel a little bit overwhelmed by the crowds. Now if you're looking for something a little more chilled after exploring the busy and loud districts of Buenos Aires then we've got the perfect day trip for you. Tigre is a town in the north of the city's metropolitan area and it is a gateway to the vast area of waterways that make up the Parana Delta. It's a very popular weekend getaway for locals, who bring their picnics and take the boats out to some of the beaches within the Delta, as well as families visiting the Parque de la Costa theme park. However it's a great day trip for tourists too and provides a very different experience from the city proper. Getting there from the center of Buenos Aires is very simple. First get yourself to Retiro's train station and look for the departures to Tigre. They go like every 30 minutes. There are two trains though: the direct one that travels inland and doesn't require you to change train and the indirect, more scenic train that travels along the coast but which requires you to change at Bartolome Mitre station. We actually took the direct train to Tigre and then the scenic one on the way back. Overall there wasn't much difference in terms on views unless you want to take things a little bit slower and get on and off at certain stations along the coast. If we were to compare Tigre to anywhere it would be the south of England, which isn't surprising given the influence the English had on this area in the past. Huge elaborate rowing clubs line the banks of the Tigre river, exclusive member-only clubs that cater for all sorts of sports, including rowing. We took a stroll up the peaceful tree-lined costanera next to the river until we arrived at the grand building housing The Museo de Arte de Tigre. This beautiful building is a work of art in itself so, whilst we didn't go into the museum, the walk was still well worth it. On the way back to the town center we obviously stopped for ice cream at Helado Via Toscana, which is the kind of ice cream you definitely won't find in the south of England! We've noticed that Argentinians really do love their ice cream. Very much like Italian gelato style. Really delicious! We've had lots of it since we've been in the country. And we're going to have even more! Oh yeah! The Puerto de Frutos is an old port that has been converted into many souvenir shops and restaurants and is a good place to spend some time browsing at the end of your time in Tigre. As you can see there is plenty to keep you busy in Buenos Aires. We stayed there for 2 weeks and whilst we did explore at a little bit of a slower pace, there was still so much that we didn't get to do. The city is a real mix of different cultural influences and feels way more like being in Western Europe than South America. We enjoyed that aspect but probably just because we'd spent so much time in Central America recently that that sort of 'homey' feel appealed to us a little bit more. If we'd have flown there directly from Europe we probably wouldn't have enjoyed it quite as much. Our time in Buenos Aires was a great start to our Argentine adventure but there is plenty more coming your way from this massive country and trust us it only gets more and more epic! So make sure to Subscribe if you'd like to follow along on our journey through Argentina and give this video a Like if you enjoyed it or found it useful. We also have a travel blog where we go into a lot more detail about all the different places that we've visited. If you're interested in getting any more information about anywhere that you've seen, make sure to go on over and check that out. Otherwise, thanks for watching and we'll catch you back here in Argentina on the next one!

videos.transcript: Hi, I am Martijn, your guide in Buenos Aires. I am 5 days in the capital of Argentina to show you the best things to do and see. From colorful streets, the best museums, the resting place of Eva Peron to everything in between. If this Buenos Aires travel guide has given you new travel tips or maybe you just like it... ...please give a thumbs up or leave a comment under the video. That's the way you can help so other people can discover the video as well and I get to create new travel guides for you in the future. Buenos Aires is served by two airports "Ezeiza" and "Aeroparque". " Ezeiza" is located 22 kilometers outside the city center and "Aeroparque" is inside the city. To travel from "Ezeiza" to the city you can use use a bus service. The journey takes around 45 to 60 minutes and a ticket will set you back around €2. And another way to travel is by taxi. What I saw, the price is around €25 to 30. And my advice is to buy a ticket at an official authorized taxi stand which is located in the arrivals hall. Let's start the Buenos Aires Travel guide in one of the most charming parts of the city "La Boca". In English it means "the mouth" and that's because of the location of this neighborhoods at the mouth of the river over here. It was constructed by immigrants from Italy a long time ago... ...and nowadays it's the place for local artists, food, and street art. The most famous and most photograph street is El Caminito. There are different stories about why the houses are so colorful. One story suggests that the houses were built from leftover materials. People painting them in different colors to hide the poverty in this area. And nowadays it's a major tourist attraction. "La Boca" is also home to the "Boca Juniors" football club. One of the most popular and successful teams in Argentina with their iconic Stadium "La Bombonera". The matches of the "Boca Juniors" are a big deal in Buenos Aires. In a moment you know one of the best museums to visit... ...but first a major and important square in the city "Plaza de Mayo". This square played a central role in the political and social life of the country. A notable landmark is "Casa Rosada" the presidential palace. A long time ago Eva Peron, also known as Evita, stood of the balcony of Casa Rosada. Because he stood up for the working class in the country she became the most famous first lady ever. The sad thing is that she died at the age of 33 because of cancer. You can visit her resting place at a beautiful cemetery in the neighborhood Recoleta. You can walk through a labyrinth of graves. This resting place, one of the most famous in the world, is a significant cultural and historical landmark... ...showcasing impressive architecture and reflecting Argentina's rich heritage. Established in 1822, it's the final resting place of many notable figures... ...including presidents, military heroes, and writers. Eva Peron's tomb is the most visited in whole Argentina, and many people come every year to show their respect. In the past, you can visit it for free, but nowadays, they ask an entrance fee around €10. In a moment, you know more about one of the hippest districts in town and an antique market. But first, the Malba Museum. Also known as the Latin America Art Museum of Bueno Aires. It is a prominent art museum dedicated to exhibiting Latin America art from the early 20th century to the present day. Made by artists like Diego Rivera, Frida Kahlo and Antonio Bernie. When you like antiques then you certainly have to visit the "San Telmo" neighborhood with the San Telmo market. This is a national monument and this building opened in the late 80s to offer the needs from immigrants from Europe. Nowadays, it offers food, antiques, music, and everything in between. The market is open every day, but some stalls only open on the weekends. Every Sunday, you can indulge yourself with more antiques and other stuff with this local market. It opened in 1970 with 270 stalls, and nowadays it attracts more than 12,000 visitors a week. In a moment, I will show you one of the widest streets in the whole world. But first, the Palermo neighborhood. It's one of the hippest districts in town and the most famous parts are Hollywood and Soho. Palermo Hollywood got its name in the late 1990s because many film and TV companies moved there. The area blends old warehouses and low-rise buildings with cafes, modern bars, and boutique hotels. Palermo Soho in the southeast got its name because of its creative and artistic feel similar to the Soho area New York. This is really the bohemian heart of Buenos Aires. Where independent designers and artists set the tone. On the weekends, Plaza Serrano, the main square here, becomes the center of activity. Hosting pop-up markets and local fashion stalls. If you are looking for some greenery, then the Japanese gardens are a great place to go. You will find two hectares of Japanese inspired nature. This is also the place for cultural events and activities. It was built in 1967 on the occasion of the visit of the Japanese emperor and his wife. And it is the largest Japanese style garden outside Japan. Surrounded by the garden, you will find the neighborhoods Palermo and also nearby Recoleta. And that's one of the most expensive parts in town with upscale restaurants, luxury hotels, and beautiful boutiques. And a must-see is Plaza Francia. It's known for its green spaces, tall trees, and great views. It's a perfect place to relax, enjoy the outdoors, and experience local culture. In a moment, I will show you one of the most beautiful bookstores in the world... ...but first, Plaza de la República. It's a main Square in the city center and over here you find also the Obelisco. It's a monumental that stands for the city's history and cultural identity. Built in 1936, it marks the spot where the Argentina flag was first raised in the city. Today it's a popular meeting point and a symbol of the city. And behind me is Avenida de 9 Julio. With a wide of 140 meters it's one of the widest Avenues in the whole world. And from this busy place we go to a more quiet one El Ateno Grand Splendid... ...and that's a bookstore in a former theater. The Guardian placed it as the second most beautiful bookstore in the world. This venue opened in 1919 and in 2000 it was converted into a bookstore. In a moment you know more about this building which is one of the most popular attractions in the city... ...and the renovated old port. But first the Museum of Fine Arts. Also known as the Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes. The museum houses a lot of old and new art from Argentina and Europe for example. You can see paintings by famous artists like Frida Kahlo and Rembrandt van Rijn. And then one of the most famous attractions in the city and that's Teatro Colón. It is considered as one of the most beautiful opera houses in the whole world. Famous for its beautiful acoustics and architecture. You can book guided tours and you can use the link in the text under the video. The old port of the city is transformed into a new and upscale neighborhoods Puerto Madero... ...with skyscrapers and this beautiful bridge Puente de la Mujer. The bridge was designed by Santiago Calatrava and maybe you know him from the Turning Torso the skyscraper in Malmo Sweden. The old brick buildings have been transformed into chic restaurants and you also see the old cranes. It's a perfect place to stroll around also by night. One of the most important religious buildings in the city is the Catedral Metropolitana de Buenos Aires. It is famous for its stunning architecture and historical significance. The construction began in the 16th century, but it underwent several renovations and expansions over the years. It is also the final resting place of General José de San Martín. A national hero who played an important role in the country's independence. When you think of Argentina, you think of Tango, the famous and passional dance. You can visit a lot of shows over here. And when you're lucky, you can also see it on the streets. What is the best time for a visit? I think the periods between November and May. And keep in mind that the summer period can be very hot and that are the months December, January, and February. If you want to avoid the major crowds, my advice is to visit Buenos Aires in the winter period... ...and that's between June and August. Please give a thumbs up. Thank you so much when you like this Buenos Aires travel guide. And if you don't want to miss new episodes, just subscribe on my YouTube channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view on the whole world.
